pattern: Answering 'does any value have this exact frequency' fast means indexing by frequency itself, not just by value: keep a second map from occurrence-count to how many distinct values currently sit at that count.
hints: Scanning every distinct value to check its count on each hasFrequency call is too slow if the structure holds many values — track something that already answers the question directly.
Alongside a value-to-count map, keep a count-to-how-many-values-have-it map; a value's contribution to the old count must move out before it moves into the new one.

On add, increment a value's count and shift its contribution from the old bucket to the new one; on deleteOne, do the same in reverse and drop the value entirely once its count reaches zero; hasFrequency is then just a bucket lookup.

edge_inputs.description: sample: adding the same value twice gives it frequency two
sample: deleting the only occurrence empties the tracker
sample: querying before anything is added reports false
one value losing an occurrence must not blind the tracker to a different value still sitting at that frequency
deleteOne on a value that was never added is a silent no-op, not an error
value and frequency at the constraint extremes
repeatedly adding and deleting the same value must keep the frequency buckets consistent across many transitions
after a value is deleted down to zero occurrences, adding it again starts its frequency fresh at one

stress.description: long random sequence of add/deleteOne/hasFrequency over a small value alphabet so frequencies collide often
stress.note: a 20-value alphabet keeps many values collapsing onto the same frequency bucket, exercising the shared-bucket bookkeeping
